titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Ming Cho Lee Designs and Papers
titlestmt.author: by Camila Zorrilla Tessler

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

conventiondeclaration.citation: translation missing: en.enumerations.resource_finding_aid_description_rules.Describing Archives: A Content Standard

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Monday April 27, 2026 at 23:00

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Most materials are open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Ming Cho Lee and Betsy Lee, 2018-2024.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into seven series: I. Productions. II. Architecture. III. Theater Administration. IV. Instruction. V. Promotional Material. VI. Artwork. VII. Personal Papers.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Ming Cho Lee (1930-2020) was a Chinese American theatrical set designer and educator. Born in Shanghai, China in 1930, Lee came to the United States in 1949. He studied at Occidental College and the University of California at Los Angeles. Lee moved to New York to work in theatrical design in 1954, apprenticing to set designer Jo Mielziner. He began independently designing shows in 1958.

In 1962 Lee designed his first set for the New York Shakespeare Festival at the Delacorte Theatre in Central Park, and became the a part of the creative team for the Festival for sixteen productions. He continued to design for the New York and regional theatre stage, designing for over twenty Broadway shows and over three hundred theatrical, opera and ballet productions around the world. His credits included sets for the New York Shakespeare Festival, the Mark Taper Forum in Los Angeles, the Guthrie Theatre in Minneapolis, the Metropolitan Opera, the New York City Opera, the Lyric Opera of Chicago, the San Francisco Opera, American Ballet Theatre, Joffrey Ballet, Eliot Field Ballet, Hamburg State Opera in Germany, Teatro Colon in Buenos Aires, and Cloud Gate Dance Theatre in Taipei, Taiwan.

Over the years, Lee received widespread acclaim for his designs including a Helen Hayes Award, two Tony Awards, a National Medal of Arts (2002), a Drama Desk Award for Outstanding Set Design, and and Obie Award for Sustained Excellence (1995). Lee received numerous fellowships including ones from the Guggenheim and National Endowment for the Arts. In 1998, Lee was inducted into the American Theatre Hall of Fame.

From 1969 until 2017, Lee taught at the Yale School of Drama. For thirty-four of his years as a Professor at Yale, he served as either chair or co-chair of the design department. Lee has received five honorary degrees. His time at the Yale School of Drama is highlighted by his annual Clambake, a portfolio review event that brought together set design students and professional designers from across the United States.

Lee worked out of a workshop in his home and many of his models feature the work of his wife, Elizabeth (Betsy) Lee (nee Rapport), who also managed his career. Ming and Betsy married in 1955 and remained married until his death in 2020.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Ming Cho Lee Designs and Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections as they are acquired, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
This collection received a basic level of processing, including rehousing and minimal organization.

Information included in the Description of Papers note and Collection Contents section is drawn from information supplied with the collection and from an initial survey of the contents. Folder titles appearing in the contents list below are often based on those provided by the creator or previous custodian. Titles have not been verified against the contents of the folders in all cases. Otherwise, folder titles are supplied by staff during initial processing.
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Ming Cho Lee papers, which span from circa 1930-2020, contain drawings, models, schematics, professional papers, personal papers, and other papers by or relating to the life and work of Ming Cho Lee.

Professional papers consist of both Lee's work in theater as well as his work as an educator. The bulk of the material consists of documentation of productions from the whole of Lee's career, which includes models, sketches, correspondence, programs, and reviews. These include both produced and unproduced shows. Work described as "elevations" specifically refers to sketches done in scale of a set design. Models include completed models, partially completed models, and model pieces. Model scales range from 1/8" inch scale, primarily used for initial drafts of set designs, to 1/4" inch and 1/2" scale models, which represent complete set designs.

Professional papers relating to his work as an architect focuses entirely on theater buildings that he designed, as well as some work on other art buildings. Also present are documents that reflect his time as an educator, both at Yale and at various other workshops and camps that he participated in or led. Contained are correspondence, contracts, and union records that document his time in the United Scenic Artists (IATSE), also known as Local 829.
The Artwork series focuses specifically on artwork that is not connected to his work as a set designer, and contains mostly sketches.
Personal papers consist primarily of correspondence, as well as some materials related to the annual Christmas party that Ming and Betsy threw for the theater community in New York City.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Ming Cho Lee Designs and Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The Productions series is arranged alphabetically by show title; each production is arranged by date and company.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Productions series contains the accumulated materials from a bulk of productions that Lee designed. These materials include research, elevations, sketches, notes, correspondence, and architectural drafts of sets, theaters, and placements. Also included are set models, both whole and in pieces. Set models are to scale at 1/8", 1/4", and 1/2"; all models were created by Lee, Elizabeth Lee (nee Rapport), and Lee's assistants.

did.unittitle: Early work, predominantly Occidental College and University of California Los Angeles photographs

Hoffmann, Hong Kong color elevations, rejected model pieces, research

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The Architecture and Theater series is organized into two subseries: Architecture and Theater. Both are arranged chronologically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Architecutre and Theater series contains the bulk of Lee's professional activities outside of specific productions, which largely focus on developing theater buildings, union activity, and some architectural work outside of theaters, such as the Chinese Court in the Metropolitan Museum of Art, and the National Museum of African Art.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains administrative documents, correspondence, notes, and meeting minutes related to Lee's work in the theater world, including his work for Local 829, the stage designers union.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The Instruction series is arranged chronologically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains materials documenting Lee's tenure at Yale as a professor, as well as some other positions he held as a teacher, including workshops and camps. Contained are correspondence, reviews, student communication, syllabi, and lectures. Also present is research into theater programs by several universities conducted on behalf of the Guggenheim Fellowship Theater Arts program.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The Artwork series is organized chronologically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains artwork that does not relate to any particular production or theater that Lee worked on, and includes sketches, watercolors, and paintings.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The Promotional Material series is arranged chronologically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains reviews, posters, artwork, and programs that were designed with the specific intention of promoting productions and events that Lee either participated in or was related to in some capacity.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The Personal Papers series is arranged alphabetically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains primarily correspondence, but also contains photographs, notes, and party planning materials that are personal and are not professional at all in nature.

did.unittitle: Angels in America review and Ming Cho Lee profile articles
China Federation of Literary and Art Circles trip
Counselor of Embassy for Press and Cultural Affairs dinner invitation
